description: Each hunt has a cluePool of 30 clues across 3 rings (distance tiers), with difficulty 1-10 per ring. Ring 1 = farthest start, Ring 2 = middle, Ring 3 = arrives at bar. Difficulty selector picks clue by difficulty level within ring.

constraints.direction: inward — each step closer to the bar

hunts.theme: Mayors, Fires, and the Man Who Built Temple Street
hunts.narrativeArc: From a French mayor's infrastructure legacy, through the fire stations that once protected this hillside, to a bar that transforms Southern tradition with laboratory precision.

cluePool.clue: You're standing at the edge of the largest Filipino American mural in the country — 145 feet of history painted onto a park wall. Take a photo of the section that grabs you. Save it. By the time you reach the bar, you'll know why it's here.
cluePool.historicalFact: Gintong Kasaysayan, Gintong Pamana — 'A Glorious History, A Golden Legacy.' Eliseo Art Silva painted it in 1995 to compress 5,000 years of Filipino and Filipino American history into one wall. It was the first public artwork anywhere to honor Larry Itliong's role in the 1965 Delano Grape Strike — eight days before Cesar Chavez joined. The mural helped inspire the city to designate Historic Filipinotown in 2002.

cluePool.clue: In front of the mural at Unidad Park, there's an interpretive panel that identifies the historical figures depicted. Find the panel. One figure is described as a national hero of the Philippines — a poet and revolutionary executed by the Spanish. What is his name, as written on the sign?
cluePool.historicalFact: Jose Rizal was a novelist, poet, ophthalmologist, and the face of Philippine independence. Spain executed him by firing squad in Manila on December 30, 1896 — he was 35. His two novels, written in Spanish, exposed colonial abuses so effectively that the Spanish government banned them. His death ignited the Philippine Revolution.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm interpretive sign is still standing and legible, and that Jose Rizal is named on it.

cluePool.clue: In the mountains of Northern Luzon, Philippines, village elders gather in a circular space called a dap-ay to settle disputes and pass down traditions. This park has one — believed to be the first built outside the Philippines. Find it. Look at the material — it matches the Cordillera originals. What is it built from?
cluePool.historicalFact: The dap-ay connects two places: the Cordillera mountains of the Philippines and a small park in Los Angeles. In Cordillera culture, the dap-ay is where oral traditions are transmitted, harvests planned, and disputes resolved. Building one here was a way of saying the community's roots didn't stop at the Pacific.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm dap-ay is accessible and that its stone construction is visible.

cluePool.clue: The interpretive panel gives the mural's full title in two languages. The English translation is 'A Glorious History, A Golden Legacy.' Read the original title. What is the first word?
cluePool.historicalFact: 'Gintong Kasaysayan, Gintong Pamana' — the word 'gintong' means 'golden' in Tagalog. Eliseo Art Silva chose a title that works as a claim: this history isn't just important, it's precious. The mural was commissioned by the Social and Public Art Resource Center (SPARC), founded by Judy Baca, who also created the Great Wall of LA in the Tujunga Wash.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm title appears on interpretive panel in Tagalog.

cluePool.clue: I organized Filipino farmworkers to walk off the grape fields on September 8, 1965. Eight days later, Cesar Chavez joined us. History remembered him. It forgot me — for decades. Find my portrait near the park. I'm painted on a small metal box most people walk right past. What is my name?
cluePool.historicalFact: Larry Itliong led the Filipino grape strike in Delano eight days before Chavez and the NFWA joined. The combined strike lasted five years and reshaped American labor rights. Itliong's role was systematically erased — Chavez received the credit, the holiday, the schools named after him. California didn't establish Larry Itliong Day until 2015, fifty years after the strike.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Larry Itliong utility box portrait and name are readable near Beverly Blvd.

cluePool.clue: Walk to the park entrance. The walkway design was inspired by a toy that a Filipino immigrant named Pedro Flores popularized in the United States in the late 1920s. Look at the walkway's shape. What toy is it modeled after?
cluePool.historicalFact: Pedro Flores opened the Yo-Yo Manufacturing Company in Santa Barbara in 1928 — the first to mass-produce the toy in America. He didn't invent it (versions existed in the Philippines for centuries), but he turned it into a craze. Duncan bought his company in 1930, but Flores kept working as a demonstrator. The park entrance pays tribute to his role in the story.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm yo-yo-inspired walkway design is identifiable at park entrance.

cluePool.clue: The artist who painted this 145-foot mural in 1995 also designed a massive arch 27 years later — the largest Filipino cultural arch in the United States, spanning Beverly Boulevard at the other end of this neighborhood. Both the mural and the arch honor the same national hero. You already found his name on the interpretive sign. Who is the artist who created both? Find his name on the mural's credit or interpretive panel.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Eliseo Art Silva's name appears on interpretive sign or a credit plaque at the mural.
cluePool.historicalFact: Eliseo Art Silva spent 27 years building Filipino identity into the physical landscape of Historic Filipinotown. The mural (1995) was the seed — it helped the city see the neighborhood's cultural significance. The Talang Gabay Eastern Gateway (2022) was the capstone — an 82-foot steel arch that took 20 years from concept to completion. Same artist, same mission, same neighborhood.

cluePool.clue: Look at the interpretive panel in front of the mural. Multiple historical figures are identified. One led a resistance against Ferdinand Magellan in 1521 — the first recorded act of Filipino resistance against European colonization. Find his name on the panel. What is it?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Lapu-Lapu is named on interpretive sign.
cluePool.historicalFact: Lapu-Lapu killed Ferdinand Magellan at the Battle of Mactan on April 27, 1521. Magellan arrived with Spanish soldiers and demanded the island chiefs convert to Christianity. Lapu-Lapu refused. He's considered the first Filipino national hero — a warrior chief who fought colonization 375 years before Jose Rizal.

cluePool.clue: I wrote a book in 1946 called 'America Is in the Heart' — a story of Filipino farmworkers facing exploitation and racism in Depression-era California. The Saturday Evening Post printed my words alongside FDR and Lincoln. I died penniless at 42. Find my name on the interpretive panel in front of this mural. What is my last name?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Carlos Bulosan is named on interpretive panel.
cluePool.historicalFact: Carlos Bulosan wrote 'America Is in the Heart' (1946), a searing account of Filipino migrant workers facing exploitation and racism in Depression-era California. The book was so powerful the Saturday Evening Post excerpted it alongside work by FDR and Lincoln. Bulosan died penniless at 42. The mural honors him alongside Rizal and Itliong — revolutionaries of pen, field, and nation.

cluePool.clue: You've read about Jose Rizal, Lapu-Lapu, and Larry Itliong on the interpretive panel. All three are also depicted in the mural. Stand back and look at the full 145-foot painting — it tells history from left to right, ancient to modern. Of these three, which one appears closest to the right side of the mural?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm mural reads left-to-right chronologically and that Itliong is identifiable near the right end.
cluePool.historicalFact: The mural sweeps from Lapu-Lapu (1521, far left) through Rizal (1896, center) to Itliong (1965, far right). 444 years compressed into 145 feet of paint. Itliong's position at the end makes a statement: this history didn't stop. The mural was the first public artwork to honor his role in the grape strike — eight days before Chavez joined, and fifty years before California gave him a holiday.

cluePool.clue: You've arrived at one of the oldest Filipino landmarks in America. Before you read anything, look at the building in front of you — the architecture, the materials, the way it sits on the corner. Take a photo. This building changed a neighborhood.
cluePool.historicalFact: The Filipino Christian Church at 301 N Union Ave is the oldest Filipino Protestant church in the United States, founded in 1928 by young Filipino men who were barred from worshipping in white congregations. Forced to relocate repeatedly by city redevelopment, the congregation finally settled here in 1950 — and their presence pulled the entire Filipino community to this corridor, creating what became Historic Filipinotown.

cluePool.clue: Look at the church at 301 N Union Ave. The building has pointed arches and steep roof lines — the same medieval European style you'd see at Notre-Dame or Westminster Abbey (at a much smaller scale). What is that architectural style?
cluePool.historicalFact: The building is German Gothic Revival mixed with Craftsman elements — a combination that tells you it was built for someone else. This was originally the Union Avenue M.E. Church, constructed for a white Methodist congregation. The Filipino Christian Fellowship acquired it in 1950. They inherited Gothic arches that were never designed for them — and made them their own.

cluePool.clue: We crossed the Pacific to study at a Christian college. We wanted to pray, but the local churches wouldn't have us. So in 1928, we started our own congregation. We were pushed out four times before we found a home. Look at the sign at 301 N Union Ave. What country of origin is in the name?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm exterior signage includes the word 'Filipino' — exact wording needed.
cluePool.historicalFact: The name is deliberate. When it was founded in 1928, Filipino immigrants in LA couldn't worship alongside white congregants, couldn't own property, and couldn't live where they chose. Calling it the Filipino Christian Church wasn't just a description — it was a declaration of existence. It remains the oldest Filipino Protestant church in the country.

cluePool.clue: Walk to the intersection of Temple St and Silverlake Blvd. A decorative gateway structure marks the entrance to Historic Filipinotown. Look at the facade — the patterns are not random. They're based on traditional Filipino embroidery found on formal clothing. What type of motifs do you see on the gateway?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Western Gateway callado patterns are visible and identifiable from sidewalk level.
cluePool.historicalFact: The patterns are called callado embroidery — the same decorative needlework found on the Maria Clara (women's formal dress) and the Barong Tagalog (men's formal shirt). The gateway was unveiled in 2002 by then-Councilmember Eric Garcetti, marking the culmination of a 30-year campaign to get the city to officially designate Historic Filipinotown.

cluePool.clue: The Western Gateway at Temple & Silverlake (2002) marks one entrance to Historic Filipinotown. Twenty years later, a sister arch was installed at the other end of the neighborhood on Beverly Boulevard. One marks the west, one marks the east. Both were championed by the same politician — first as a councilmember, then as mayor. The Western Gateway has a name on it or near it. What neighborhood does it mark the entrance to?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm 'Historic Filipinotown' text is readable on or near the Western Gateway.
cluePool.historicalFact: Historic Filipinotown was designated in 2002 — the first Filipino community in America to receive a named area with distinct geographic boundaries. It took 30 years of organizing. The Western Gateway went up the same year. The Eastern Gateway, Talang Gabay, took another 20 years — an 82-foot steel arch designed by the same artist who painted the mural you just saw at Unidad Park.

cluePool.clue: The church at 301 N Union Ave blends Gothic arches with a distinctly American style. Look at the simpler elements — the exposed woodwork, the low-pitched details, the emphasis on natural materials. It's the same style you'd see at Pasadena's Gamble House or the bungalows of Craftsman-era California. What is that American style?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Craftsman elements (exposed woodwork, horizontal lines, natural materials) are visible on the church exterior.
cluePool.historicalFact: German Gothic Revival meets American Craftsman — a combination that reveals the building's layered identity. This was the Union Avenue M.E. Church before the Filipino congregation acquired it in 1950. The Craftsman movement prized handmade detail and natural materials — ironic for a building that was abandoned by one community and adopted by another.

cluePool.clue: The Filipino Christian Church is a designated Los Angeles Historic-Cultural Monument. Look for a plaque or marker on or near the building. What HCM number is it?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm HCM plaque is visible from street and that #651 is readable.
cluePool.historicalFact: HCM #651, designated in 1998. Twenty-one years later, in 2019, the church was also listed on the National Register of Historic Places by the National Park Service. It is the only historic cultural monument designated by the City of Los Angeles with Filipino origins — a distinction that says as much about what the city overlooked as what it chose to honor.

cluePool.clue: The dap-ay at Unidad Park (Ring 1) and the lamp post medallions near this church both embed Filipino values into physical infrastructure. Find a medallion on one of the lamp posts near the church. Three Tagalog words are encoded in the designs — one means 'shared humanity,' one means 'journey,' one means 'peace.' The word for shared humanity starts with 'K.' What is it?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm lamp post medallions are present near the church intersection and that 'kapwa' text is readable on them.
cluePool.historicalFact: Kapwa, lakbay, kapayapaan — shared humanity, journey, peace. 54 lamp posts across 17 intersections, designed by Roel Punzalan, unveiled in October 2016. The dap-ay in the park encodes community through architecture. The medallions encode it through street furniture. Both say the same thing in different materials: this neighborhood belongs to us.

cluePool.clue: You found 'kapwa' on the lamp post medallion. Two other Tagalog words are encoded in the designs nearby. One means 'journey.' One means 'peace.' Find the word for 'journey.' What is it?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm 'lakbay' text appears on lamp post medallions near the church.
cluePool.historicalFact: Lakbay — journey. The word choice is precise. For the Filipino community in LA, the journey was literal: across the Pacific, through discriminatory housing laws, past church doors that wouldn't open. The 54 lamp posts mark the territory that journey created. Each medallion is a claim staked in municipal infrastructure — permanent, public, and in Tagalog.

cluePool.clue: Three Tagalog words on the lamp posts. A dap-ay in the park. Callado embroidery on the gateway. A mural spanning 145 feet. All were built within a 0.4-mile radius. The HCM plaque on the church tells you when this building was designated a landmark. The gateway tells you when the neighborhood was officially named. Read both. Which came first — the landmark designation or the neighborhood name?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm HCM plaque shows 1998 and gateway shows 2002. Church landmark (1998) came 4 years before HiFi designation (2002).
cluePool.historicalFact: The church became HCM #651 in 1998. Historic Filipinotown was designated in 2002. The landmark came first — proof that the community existed before the city acknowledged it. The mural (1995) preceded both. Eliseo Art Silva painted the neighborhood's identity before the government drew its boundaries.

cluePool.clue: You're almost at your destination. But first — look up. A 14-foot neon giraffe is watching you from the side of a building. Take a photo with the giraffe. This is the one you send ahead to whoever's waiting at the bar.
cluePool.historicalFact: The neon giraffe has been on this building since it was a deli. When three stop-motion animators moved their studio here in 2003, they kept the giraffe, restored the neon, and changed the word at the bottom from 'DELI' to 'Hi-Fi' — the neighborhood abbreviation for Historic Filipinotown. The studio inside created Robot Chicken and Moral Orel for Adult Swim.

cluePool.clue: Find the 14-foot neon giraffe sign near Temple St and Belmont Ave. At the bottom of the sign, four letters spell out a word. What does it say?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm neon giraffe sign currently reads 'Hi-Fi' at the bottom.
cluePool.historicalFact: The sign originally said 'DELI' when this was a neighborhood delicatessen. The stop-motion animation studio Screen Novelties moved in and swapped it to 'Hi-Fi' — short for Historic Filipinotown. It's a double meaning: the neighborhood name, and a nod to high-fidelity sound, fitting for a studio that obsesses over detail in every frame.

cluePool.clue: The neon giraffe sign hangs on a building that houses a stop-motion animation studio — the studio behind Robot Chicken. Look closely at the giraffe. The artist included an X-ray view of the giraffe's neck, revealing its interior — the kind of detail only animators would obsess over. What color is the neon that shows the inside of the neck?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm X-ray neck detail is visible and gold-colored neon.
cluePool.historicalFact: The X-ray giraffe neck connects the sign to the studio inside: Screen Novelties builds puppets and sets where every detail matters. The gold neon skeletal interior is the kind of thing most people walk past without noticing — but stop-motion animators see skeletons inside everything. They build armatures (metal skeletons) inside every puppet they make.

cluePool.clue: The building with the neon giraffe houses an animation studio. Look at the building itself — not the sign. What material is the building made of?
cluePool.historicalFact: Screen Novelties — formed in 2003 by Mark Caballero, Seamus Walsh, and Christopher Finnegan — were among the first stop-motion artists to adopt entirely digital capture systems. Their work launched both Robot Chicken and Moral Orel for Adult Swim. Atlas Obscura calls it 'a puppet-lover's paradise.'

cluePool.clue: I was built in 1913 as two separate shops: a market and a tailor. A man who fled a genocide in 1914 set up in my other half. In 2001, Hollywood turned me into a family store owned by a street racer. Find me at the corner of Bellevue and Edgeware. What name is on my sign today?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Bob's Market exterior signage is visible and readable.
cluePool.historicalFact: Architect George F. Colterison designed this building in 1913 — a market on one side, a tailor on the other. The tailor, Levon Melkonian, fled the Armenian Genocide in 1914. The two shops combined in 1934. The Nimura family named it Bob's Market in 1965. And in 2001, it became Toretto's Market in The Fast and the Furious — Vin Diesel's family store. Four lives in one building.

cluePool.clue: Bob's Market at Bellevue & Edgeware has decorative arched details and ornamental trim above the storefront — the same style you'd see on Spanish missions along the California coast and at Stanford University's Main Quad. What architectural style is it?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Mission Revival architectural elements are visible from street.
cluePool.historicalFact: Bob's Market has Mission Revival and Oriental architectural elements on a one-story false-front frame — designed by George F. Colterison in 1913. The building housed two shops: a market and a tailor. The tailor, Levon Melkonian, had fled the Armenian Genocide. In 2001, Vin Diesel's character parked out front. The building outlived everyone's plans for it.

cluePool.clue: Walk to the 1300 block of Carroll Avenue. You're looking at the finest collection of Victorian houses remaining in Los Angeles. One of these houses appeared as a manor where three sisters practiced witchcraft for eight seasons on TV. The show ran from 1998 to 2006. Find the house — it stands out. What is the street number on it?
cluePool.historicalFact: The Innes House at 1329 Carroll Ave was built for city councilman and real estate developer Daniel Innes in the late 1880s. A century later, it became the Halliwell Manor on Charmed (1998-2006). The real story is why it's still standing: the banking recession of 1888 halted construction across LA. Developers were too broke to tear down and rebuild. The crash that killed the land boom is the reason these Victorians survived.

cluePool.clue: I learned to walk backward before I could moonwalk forward. In 1983, I filmed the most famous music video ever made — the final scene was shot in a house on this block. I changed music videos forever. Find the house. What is my name?
cluePool.historicalFact: The Sanders House at 1345 Carroll Ave appeared in the final scene of Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' video — the most influential music video ever made. Three pop culture franchises filmed within 400 meters of each other on this block: Thriller (1983), Charmed (1998), and The Fast and the Furious (2001). Angelino Heights became Hollywood's backlot without trying.
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm 1345 Carroll Ave is identifiable as the Thriller house from the street.

cluePool.clue: Three buildings on your walk got famous second lives: the deli became an animation studio (the giraffe sign), the grocery store became a movie set (Bob's Market / Toretto's), and the Victorian became a witch's manor (Charmed house). All three kept their original facades. Look at all three buildings. Two are made of wood. One is not. Which building is built from a different material — and what is that material?
cluePool.historicalFact: Bob's Market: wood-frame false front (1913). The Charmed house: wood Victorian (1880s). The giraffe building: brick (date unknown). Three buildings, three eras, three materials — but only one needed brick to survive a century on a commercial street. The Victorians survived because a recession stopped developers from tearing them down. The brick building survived because brick just lasts.

cluePool.clue: Your walk started at a mural honoring Filipino farmworkers and ended at Victorian houses used as Hollywood sets. Between them: a church that created a neighborhood, lamp posts that speak Tagalog, and a neon giraffe that marks an animation studio. One artist connects the beginning to the end of your route — the same person who painted the mural also designed the arch on Beverly Boulevard. You already found his name. Now: the neon giraffe sign used to say 'DELI.' It was changed to 'Hi-Fi' when the studio moved in. What does 'Hi-Fi' stand for in this neighborhood?
cluePool.historicalFact: Hi-Fi is the neighborhood abbreviation for Historic Filipinotown — designated in 2002, three decades after the Filipino community rooted itself here. The neon giraffe's text swap from DELI to Hi-Fi is a one-word history of gentrification: a neighborhood deli becomes a boutique animation studio, but the new tenants honor the community they moved into. The whole walk you just took — mural, church, gateway, lamp posts, giraffe — is a community writing itself into the physical landscape so it can't be erased.

bar.name: Seven Grand
bar.vibe: Hunting-lodge whiskey bar with 700+ bottles. Taxidermy. Live jazz.

hunts.theme: Charity, Grasshoppers, and the Girl Named Spring
hunts.narrativeArc: A street ashamed of its name. A street named for a woman who may not have existed. And a whiskey bar sitting on top of a map drawn before any of these streets were real.
avoidZones: Stay within the DTLA grid — no freeway crossings needed

cluePool.clue: You're standing at the corner where Route 66 ended — 2,448 miles of America, terminating right here on Broadway. This block has more movie palaces per foot than anywhere else on earth. Look around. Take a photo of whatever catches your eye first. Save it — you'll want to compare it to where you end up.
cluePool.historicalFact: From 1926 to 1936, 7th & Broadway was the official western terminus of US Route 66. Dust Bowl migrants, dreamers, and drifters who drove 2,448 miles from Chicago ended their journey at this intersection — surrounded by movie palaces, department stores, and 504,000 pedestrians a day.

cluePool.clue: Find the sidewalk marker at the corner of 7th and Broadway. A historic plaque marks the end point of a famous road. What is the road's number?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Route 66 sidewalk marker is still present and legible at 7th & Broadway.
cluePool.historicalFact: The Hampton Hotels Save-A-Landmark program installed this marker in 2003. The original western terminus of Route 66 sat at this corner from 1926 to 1936 — before the route was extended to Santa Monica. The famous pier sign came later. This was the real end of the road.

cluePool.clue: Look up at 615 S Broadway. A vertical blade sign spells out a city name in large letters on the facade. What does it say?
cluePool.historicalFact: The Los Angeles Theatre opened January 30, 1931 — the last and most extravagant of 12 movie palaces built on Broadway between 1911 and 1931. Charlie Chaplin helped finance its completion so it could open in time to premiere his film 'City Lights.' His guests on opening night: Albert Einstein and his wife Elsa.

cluePool.clue: Stand on Broadway between 3rd and 9th Streets. You're in the densest concentration of historic theaters in the world. Count the ornate theater marquees visible from street level on this stretch. How many movie palaces were built on this strip between 1911 and 1931?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm that signage, plaques, or Broadway Historic Theatre District markers reference the count of 12 movie palaces on-site.
cluePool.historicalFact: Twelve ornate movie palaces were built on Broadway between 1911 and 1931 — the Palace (1911), the Million Dollar (1918), the State (1921), the Tower (1927), the Los Angeles (1931), and seven more. It's the first and largest historic theater district listed on the National Register, added in 1979.

cluePool.clue: I made a tramp famous. I made the world cry without saying a word. In January 1931, I needed a theater for my new film — so I helped pay to finish one. My guest that night won a Nobel Prize for explaining how light bends. Find the theater at 615 S Broadway. What style is the facade — French ___?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm that 'French Baroque' or 'Baroque' appears on any exterior plaque, HCM marker, or signage at the Los Angeles Theatre.
cluePool.historicalFact: Charlie Chaplin helped finance the completion of the Los Angeles Theatre so it could open in time to premiere 'City Lights' on January 30, 1931. He brought Albert Einstein as his date. The theater was designed by S. Charles Lee in French Baroque style — its lobby modeled after the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles.

cluePool.clue: At 648 S Broadway, a sign spells out a name above a multi-story facade. The business behind that sign fed 10,000 people for free during the Great Depression. What name is on the sign?
cluePool.historicalFact: Clifford Clinton opened Clifton's Cafeteria in 1931 as 'a poor man's nightclub.' His business policy, printed on every check: 'Our cashier will cheerfully accept whatever you wish to pay — or you may dine free.' During a 90-day period, 10,000 people ate without paying. Clinton later helped bust an LAPD corruption ring. Someone planted a bomb in his basement as thanks.

cluePool.clue: Clifton's Cafeteria at 648 S Broadway once ran a second location — Clifton's Silver Spoon — in a jewelry emporium building at 515 W 7th St. That building is your final destination tonight. The Silver Spoon's decorating style left something behind in the new tenant's space. Walk into the bar later and look for mounted animals on the walls. What kind of decorations are they?
cluePool.historicalFact: Clifton's Silver Spoon Cafeteria occupied the Brock & Company building at 515 W 7th from 1974 until Cedd Moses opened Seven Grand there in 2007. The taxidermy throughout Seven Grand descends from the Clifton's aesthetic — a hunting-lodge-meets-cafeteria style that became the whiskey bar's signature.

cluePool.clue: Walk to 636 S Broadway — an ornate theater facade with the word PALACE on its marquee. This is the oldest surviving Orpheum theater in the United States, opened in 1911. Harry Houdini, Will Rogers, and Fred Astaire all performed on its stage. Look at the marquee. What single word is displayed in the largest letters?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm PALACE marquee text is visible and legible from street level at 636 S Broadway.
cluePool.historicalFact: The Palace Theatre opened in 1911 as part of the Orpheum vaudeville circuit — Harry Houdini performed escape acts on this stage. When sound killed vaudeville, it became the Broadway Palace silent movie house in 1926, then a Fox West Coast Theatre in 1929. A million-dollar restoration in 2010 brought it back as a live venue. The oldest of Broadway's 12 palaces, and the only one that survived as a performance space.

cluePool.clue: The Los Angeles Theatre facade has ornate detailing in a specific material above its entrance and along its Broadway frontage — the same material used on dozens of DTLA buildings from this era, fired and glazed into decorative panels. Look at the facade. What is the material?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm terra cotta detailing is visible and identifiable on the Los Angeles Theatre facade from street level.
cluePool.historicalFact: Glazed terra cotta was the defining material of DTLA's golden age. Cheaper than carved stone but capable of intricate ornamentation, it let architects like S. Charles Lee create French Baroque fantasies on Broadway budgets. The Los Angeles Theatre's terra cotta facade has survived 95 years of sun, smog, and earthquakes.

cluePool.clue: Two buildings on this block opened during the same crisis: the Los Angeles Theatre at 615 S Broadway (1931) and Clifton's Cafeteria at 648 S Broadway (1931). One sold fantasy — a French Baroque lobby modeled after Versailles. The other sold survival — free meals, no questions asked. Both are still standing. Look at both facades. The theater's facade uses glazed terra cotta. Clifton's facade has a different look — more organic, with forest and nature elements visible from the street. What natural feature dominates Clifton's exterior design?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm nature/forest theming is visible on Clifton's exterior facade from street level at 648 S Broadway.
cluePool.historicalFact: Both buildings opened in 1931 — the worst year of the Great Depression. The Los Angeles Theatre offered escape: a 2,000-seat palace where you forgot you were broke. Clifton's offered sustenance: a cafeteria where you ate whether you could pay or not. The theater's lobby was modeled after Versailles. Clifton's interior was modeled after the Santa Cruz Mountains — rock sculptor Francois Scotti built a 20-foot waterfall and faux redwood trees that concealed the steel columns. One building hid your poverty in glamour, the other hid it in nature.

cluePool.clue: You've walked from Broadway to Olive Street — from the entertainment strip to the money strip. The building in front of you was designed to make bankers feel underdressed. Look at the entrance. Take a photo of the doors. Send it ahead — they won't believe these are real.
cluePool.historicalFact: The James Oviatt Building at 617 S Olive was built in 1928 by LA's most fashionable haberdasher. He visited the 1925 Paris Exposition, saw Rene Lalique's glass pavilion, and commissioned the artisan's first commercial installation: 30 tons of crystal glass shipped from France. The front doors alone are the largest panels of lead crystal Lalique ever produced.

cluePool.clue: Look at the front doors of the building at 617 S Olive St. Two figures are carved into the glass panels. What are the two figures holding between them?
cluePool.verificationNeeded: Confirm Lalique glass doors with two angels holding a mission bell are visible from street without entering the building.
cluePool.historicalFact: Rene Lalique carved two angels holding a California mission bell into lead crystal — each door panel 7.5 feet tall. Marcus Priteca gave every surface a pattern. The interior murals, by Anthony B. Heinsbergen, are even more elaborate — a polychromatic fan-vaulted lobby in gold and henna.

cluePool.clue: One man owned both the theater at 6233 Hollywood Blvd and the bar next door at 6245 Hollywood Blvd from 1949 to 1954. He was an aviator, film producer, and billionaire. cluePool.historicalFact: Howard Hughes owned both the Pantages Theatre and the Frolic Room from 1949 to 1954. He installed the neon sign that still hangs outside the bar. cluePool.clue: Look at the facade material of the Pantages Theatre. The Art Deco patterns aren't painted on — they're carved into a specific material. hunts.theme: Chaplin, McPherson, and the Lake That Echoes
hunts.narrativeArc: From a megachurch where a woman faked her own kidnapping, past the studio where movies were invented before Hollywood existed, to a lake named for the sounds bouncing off its reservoir walls — and a bar with nothing to prove.

cluePool.clue: You're about to walk through a century of Echo Park history. The path starts at a dome and ends at a dive bar. Take a photo of yourself here — save it. You'll want to compare it to the one at the end.

cluePool.clue: Find the cornerstone of the domed church at 1100 Glendale Blvd. The inscription begins 'Dedicated unto the cause of...' What is the very last word of the inscription?

His bust sits at Echo Park Lake because this neighborhood became the heart of LA's Cuban exile community after 1962 — when a local Presbyterian church met the first flight of Cuban refugees at the airport.

cluePool.clue: I was the first woman to preach on the radio. I drew 5,300 every Sunday. In 1926, I disappeared from a beach and a city mourned. Find my temple at 1100 Glendale Blvd — read the cornerstone. How many words are in the full inscription?
cluePool.historicalFact: Aimee Semple McPherson opened Angelus Temple on New Year's Day, 1923. She pioneered 'illustrated sermons' — full theatrical productions with sets, costumes, and lighting that packed all 5,300 seats. When she vanished from Venice Beach in 1926, two people died searching for her. She reappeared five weeks later walking out of the Mexican desert, claiming kidnapping. Witnesses placed her in a Carmel cottage with her married radio engineer.

cluePool.clue: The bronze bust near the lake carries two inscriptions in Spanish. The longer one begins with 'El que tenga...' Find the shorter inscription. What is its first word?
cluePool.historicalFact: 'Con todos y para el bien de todos' — 'With all, and for the good of all.' Marti wrote that line in 1891 while organizing a revolution from a New York apartment. He died in battle four years later, on horseback at Dos Rios. He was 42.

cluePool.clue: Born in Havana. Imprisoned at 17 for advocating independence. Organized a revolution from exile. Died on horseback in the first battle. Find his bust near Echo Park Lake — the longer inscription contains one Spanish word meaning 'homeland.' What is it?
cluePool.historicalFact: The full inscription reads: 'El que tenga Patria que la honre, el que no, que la conquiste' — 'He who has a homeland, honor it; he who does not, conquer one.' After 1962, Echo Park became the social and cultural heart of LA's Cuban exile community. The sculptor, Sergio Lopez Mesa, was born in Havana and studied in Rome.

cluePool.clue: The domed church at 1100 Glendale Blvd was built by a Canadian woman in 1923. The bust near the lake was placed by Cuban exiles in 1976. Both are declarations carved into permanent materials by communities of newcomers. Read the longer inscription on the bust — it ends with an imperative verb in Spanish, a command to those without a homeland. What is the last word of that inscription?
cluePool.historicalFact: 'El que tenga Patria que la honre, el que no, que la conquiste' — 'He who has a homeland, honor it; he who does not, conquer one.' McPherson carved her mission into a cornerstone. The Cuban community carved their rallying cry into a pedestal. Fifty-three years apart, same neighborhood, same impulse: write it in stone so it lasts.

cluePool.clue: The cornerstone inscription contains two compound words. One is 'worldwide' — easy to spot. The other is hyphenated and harder to notice. What is the hyphenated word?
cluePool.historicalFact: McPherson's vision was inter-denominational — she didn't care what church you came from, as long as you showed up. At a time when Protestant denominations fought over doctrine, she built a 5,300-seat tent that welcomed everyone. cluePool.clue: The cornerstone inscription at the temple and the longer inscription on the bust near the lake were carved 53 years apart. cluePool.historicalFact: This lake started as a reservoir in 1868, feeding a woolen factory at 6th and Figueroa — miles from here. A parks superintendent converted it to recreation in 1892 after he shouted across the water and heard his own voice come back. He named the park after the sound.

cluePool.clue: I was a parks superintendent inspecting a new reservoir in 1892. I shouted across the water and heard my own voice come back. I was so charmed that I named the entire park after what I heard. What did I hear?
cluePool.historicalFact: Joseph Henry Tomlinson heard echoes bouncing off the reservoir walls and named his park accordingly. The irony: as Echo Park urbanized, the echoes faded. The acoustic phenomenon that gave the neighborhood its name hasn't been heard in decades.

cluePool.clue: Look at the pedestal panels on the statue. Each depicts a different LA landmark. One shows a venue where orchestras perform outdoors. One shows a place where ships dock. One shows a mountain range. One shows a building full of books. Name the building full of books.

cluePool.clue: The temple at 1100 Glendale Blvd was built by a woman from Ontario, Canada. The statue at the lake was carved by a woman from Hilo, Hawaii. cluePool.clue: The building at 1706 W Sunset was built by a man who manufactured one material — the material that LA needed more of than anything as the city expanded in the early 1900s. He used that same material to build this recreation center. Look at the building. What material is it made of?
cluePool.historicalFact: Henry Christian Jensen manufactured bricks for LA's rapid expansion — his product literally built the city. hunts.theme: The Man Who Walked to LA and the Street Named for a Ghost
hunts.narrativeArc: A man walked 3,507 miles from Ohio to build a museum with a tunnel cut into rock. A Prohibition bowling alley hid its whiskey behind doctors' prescriptions. A governor who never visited LA left his name on a 28-mile street. And at the end: a cave.

cluePool.historicalFact: The walk ahead covers half a mile of Figueroa — Highland Park's commercial spine since the 1920s. You'll pass a 1925 movie palace, a Prohibition-era bowling alley, and a 75-foot Chicano mural before arriving at a bar built behind 100-year-old church doors from Mexico.

cluePool.clue: The giant figure on the rooftop at 5558 N Figueroa is holding something in its hand. What is it holding?

cluePool.clue: Now look down. The sidewalk near 5558 N Figueroa has tile mosaics embedded in the concrete — most people walk right over them. Find the mosaic closest to the rooftop statue. What does it depict?

cluePool.clue: Read the signage on the building beneath the rooftop figure. A business operates at street level. What is the name of the studio?

Governor Schwarzenegger gave the statue a Governor's Historic Preservation Award in 2010. A fried chicken mascot, preserved for history.

cluePool.clue: I stood on Broadway in downtown LA for 20 years, selling fried chicken to anyone who walked by. When my owner died in 1984, I was homeless. A woman put me in storage for 23 years before giving me a new rooftop in Highland Park. What am I made of? Look closely — I'm not metal.

cluePool.clue: Walk to the corner of Avenue 56 and Figueroa. A three-story building stands on the corner — built in 1923 for a secret society that met on the second floor. The building sat empty, became a wedding venue, and in 2017 reopened as Lodge Room — a 500-capacity music venue. The original cherry wood paneling and Masonic paintings are still inside.

cluePool.historicalFact: Streetcars ran down Figueroa through the 1950s. The Highland Theatre is still standing three blocks south — but the trolleys are gone and the theater closed in 2024, six days short of its 100th anniversary.

cluePool.clue: One of the sidewalk mosaics depicts a group of women standing in front of a building — members of a club founded in 1903 that organized for women's suffrage before California granted the vote in 1911.
